description: This is minified third-party vendor code (dat.GUI) where `c` is a hardcoded property name from internal call sites (e.g. "r", "g", "b", "h", "s", "v"), not attacker-controlled input; the bracket assignment `b.__state[c]=...` is a color-space conversion, not a prototype pollution sink reachable from untrusted data.

The regex /([a-z]+)(\d+)/ has no nested/overlapping quantifiers or ambiguous alternation, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking; it matches in linear time regardless of input. This is a bundled third-party library (three.js) and the pattern itself is not vulnerable, so the ReDoS classification is a mis-detection.

This is vendored three.js animation code indexing morphTargetInfluences with a numeric frame counter (animation.lastFrame), not an attacker-controlled key string like '__proto__'/'constructor'/'prototype'; there is no dynamic object property assignment here, so the prototype-pollution classification is a mistake by the tool.

This is data seeding code (challenge/data creator) where `challenge.key` comes from static internal challenge definitions bulk-inserted into the DB, not from untrusted user input; the bracket assignment is a generic pattern match, not exploitable prototype pollution.

User-controlled req.query.q is only length-truncated (no escaping/parameterization) and is directly string-concatenated into a raw SQL query executed via sequelize.query, allowing injection e.g. via a crafted 'q' value containing quote characters.

Before reaching the sink, criteria is rejected if it contains any quote character, semicolon, '#', or 'and'/'or' (case-insensitive), which blocks the string-literal breakout needed for injection; the literal '%' padding around ${criteria} in the query also prevents a trailing-backslash escape of the closing quote. The taint tool flags the sink without recognizing this blacklist as an effective sanitizer for this specific query shape.

req.body.email and req.body.password are concatenated directly into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query; the preceding regex check is a bypassable blacklist and doesn't even halt execution (no return after res.send), so the tainted query still runs regardless, making this exploitable SQL injection.

The email parameter is safely bound via `$1`/`bind`, and the password is passed through `security.hash()` (SHA-256 hex digest) before string interpolation, so the interpolated value can only contain hex characters and cannot break out of the quoted string to inject SQL.

req.body.email is concatenated directly into a raw SQL string; the regex check on line 15 is trivially bypassable (blocks only ['-;] literally) and even when it matches, execution falls through to the query anyway since there's no return/else after res.status(451).send(). This is the well-known Juice Shop login SQL injection challenge and is exploitable.

The email parameter (req.body.email) is passed via Sequelize's parameterized `replacements`, not string-concatenated, so it cannot inject SQL. The password value is first passed through `security.hash()`, and typical hash functions produce fixed-format hex output with no SQL metacharacters, so the template-literal interpolation of the hash result is not attacker-controllable in a way that enables injection.

req.body.email is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string via template literal with no escaping/parameterization before being passed to sequelize.query, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g., login bypass via ' OR '1'='1); this is the known intentionally-vulnerable Juice Shop login pattern.

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query without parameterization or escaping, allowing classic authentication-bypass/UNION-based SQL injection (e.g. email = ' OR '1'='1).

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string via template literals; the preceding regex check on line 15 only sends a 451 response without a return/next(), so execution falls through to the vulnerable query regardless, and even if it did block, blacklist-style quote/dash/semicolon matching is trivially bypassable.

User-controlled `criteria` from req.query.q is interpolated directly into a raw SQL string via template literal; the `.replace()` call on line 5 doesn't mutate the string in place and its result is discarded, so no sanitization actually occurs before the tainted value reaches the sequelize.query sink.

The guard on line 6 uses `!startsWith('apple') || !startsWith('orange')`, which by De Morgan's law is equivalent to `!(startsWith('apple') && startsWith('orange'))` — since no string can start with both prefixes simultaneously, this condition is always true, so the function always returns 400 before line 10 executes. The tainted 'criteria' therefore never reaches the sql sink for any input, making the reported flow unreachable despite the underlying string-interpolation pattern being a latent SQ

Assigning to quantityMap[item.ProductId] only sets a property (or at worst __proto__) on this single local component instance object, not a shared/global object merged via a generic deep-merge; it does not pollute Object.prototype or any object reachable elsewhere, so it's not exploitable prototype pollution.

This is a Jasmine/Jest unit test assigning a dummy placeholder string ('test-private-key') to a component property to exercise error-handling logic; it is test fixture data, not a real hardcoded cryptographic key or secret with any production exposure.

The pattern `^(?=.*?[...])` contains a single unnested quantifier (`.*?`) followed by one character class with no overlapping/ambiguous alternation, so matching is linear in input length and cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking regardless of input; it's also applied to a password field bounded in practice.
This is a value assigned in an Angular unit test to verify client-side password-match form validation logic, not a credential used for authentication or stored anywhere sensitive.

This is test/mock code in an Angular spec file assigning a mock socket event handler function; there is no dynamic property assignment from untrusted input or merge/clone operation that could pollute Object.prototype.

The `phase` key is statically typed as `Phase` (a fixed union like 'find it' | 'fix it'), not an arbitrary attacker-controlled string, so `solves[challengeKey][phase] = verdict` cannot be used to set `__proto__` or other polluting keys; this is a generic bracket-assignment pattern match, not exploitable prototype pollution.

The regex /[/#]{0,2} vuln-code-snippet start (?<challenges>.*)/g has no nested/overlapping quantifiers or ambiguous alternation; {0,2} is a small bounded repetition and .* is a single linear scan, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking regardless of input size or content.

jwt.verify() is called on user-controlled token without restricting allowed algorithms, so an attacker who knows the RSA public key can forge a token signed with HS256 using that key as the HMAC secret, causing decoded (attacker-controlled) data to be trusted and cached as an authenticated user identity — a classic JWT algorithm-confusion authorization bypass.

This is a simple object property assignment (obj[key] = value) on server-controlled data (a JWT token and a database-generated user id), not a recursive merge of attacker-supplied JSON into an object — the actual prototype-pollution pattern. Even if token were '__proto__', it would only alter tokenMap's own prototype, not Object.prototype globally, so there's no genuine cross-object pollution primitive here.

The shown code only returns a cached HMAC key sourced from an environment variable or local file and is consumed by crypto.createHmac in ctfFlag; there is no HTML sink or user-controlled input in this snippet, so the 'flows to html sink' claim does not match the code shown.

The regex /(\r\n|\n|\r)/gm has no nested quantifiers or overlapping alternations that can cause catastrophic backtracking; each alternative matches a fixed, non-overlapping 1-2 character sequence, so matching is linear in input length regardless of content.

The value is JSON.stringify-encoded and written as an SSE data frame (res.write with `data: ...\n\n`), not inserted into an HTML document or response with an HTML content-type; JSON.stringify escapes quotes/control chars appropriately for this sink, so there's no server-side HTML injection here regardless of what a client does with the delta text.

The sink at line 18 is a lookup into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get(...)), not any network/HTTP request; there is no outbound request constructed from the cookie value, so this cannot be SSRF regardless of taint from req.cookies.

baseUser[field] is a single-level bracket assignment on a plain object where field is a bare string from splitting a query param (no dotted/nested path), so even if field were '__proto__' or 'constructor' it can only rebind baseUser's own prototype/property locally—it never traverses into a nested object or merges attacker-controlled values into Object.prototype, so there's no global pollution vector here.

req.cookies.token is only used as a lookup key into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get), not passed to any network/URL-fetching sink, so this is a rule mis-classification with no actual SSRF.

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query with no parameterization or escaping, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g. auth bypass via ' OR '1'='1).

The regex /(?<major>\d+).(?<minor>\d+).(?<patch>\d+)/ has no nested/overlapping quantifiers so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking, and its input is utils.version() — an internal, non-attacker-controlled string — not user input, so it isn't reachable as a DoS vector.

BasketModel is a Sequelize (SQL) model, not a NoSQL store, and req.params.id from Express routing is always a plain string (unlike req.query/req.body which can be parsed into nested objects), so it cannot be crafted into an operator-injection object like {$ne: null}; Sequelize will safely parameterize the equality check.

req.cookies.token is used only as a lookup key into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get()) to retrieve the current session's user; it is never used to construct a URL or make an outbound request, so there is no SSRF sink here at all — a clear mis-classification by the tool.
The redirect target is server-controlled (process.env.BASE_PATH), not user-controllable request data, so there is no attacker-influenced input flowing to the redirect sink despite the taint label.

process.env.BASE_PATH is a server-side configuration value set by the deployment operator, not attacker-controlled input, so there is no untrusted taint reaching the redirect sink despite the tool's generic tainting of process.env.

Line 21 only uses req.cookies.token as a lookup key into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get), not as input to the fetch() SSRF sink; the actual sink at line 24 uses `url` derived from req.body.imageUrl, an unrelated taint source. The tool appears to have mis-attributed the sink location/taint source.

The file path uses loggedInUser.data.id (from an authenticated session lookup, not the tainted url) and ext, which is constrained to a hardcoded whitelist ['jpg','jpeg','png','svg','gif'] with a safe default — no attacker-controlled path segments reach the fs.createWriteStream call, so path traversal isn't possible here even though 'url' is tainted (the real risk is SSRF via fetch(url), a different CWE class).
User-controlled req.query.q (only length-limited, not escaped/parameterized) is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string executed via sequelize.query, allowing classic UNION-based SQL injection.

The 'sink' is security.authenticatedUsers.get(req.cookies.token), a lookup in an in-memory session map, not a network request; there is no outbound HTTP call using tainted data, so this cannot be SSRF. The tool mis-classified a simple map lookup as an SSRF sink.

process.env.BASE_PATH is a server-side configuration value set by the deployment operator, not attacker-controlled input reachable via HTTP request; treating process.env as a generic taint source misclassifies trusted server config as tainted data.

req.cookies.token is only used as a key into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get), not passed to any network request or SSRF sink; this is a mis-tagged finding with no outbound request present.

`key` comes directly from `req.body.key` (untrusted user input) and is concatenated unsanitized into a filesystem path passed to `fs.existsSync`/`fs.readFileSync`, allowing path traversal (e.g. `key=../../../etc/passwd%00` or similar) to read arbitrary `.info.yml`-suffixed files outside the intended directory.

The tainted 'key' only influences which local .yml filename is read from a fixed codefixes directory (potential path traversal, CWE-22), not the YAML content passed to yaml.load; since js-yaml's load() in v4 uses the safe default schema and the deserialized data itself is not attacker-controlled, there's no exploitable deserialization sink here.

`key` is used to index into a persistent, module-scoped `CodeFixes` cache object with no allowlist/sanitization shown, and appears to originate directly from a request parameter (per the `FixesRequestParams`/`VerdictRequestBody` interfaces below). If an attacker supplies key="__proto__", `CodeFixes[key] = {...}` reassigns CodeFixes's own prototype link, corrupting a shared server-side object (potential DoS/behavior break for all subsequent requests) — a narrower but still real instance of the un

The regex /_(\d+)/ is a simple linear pattern with a single non-nested quantifier and no overlapping alternations or ambiguous repetition, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking regardless of input; this is a mis-classification by the tool.

This is a test fixture in a *.test.ts file creating a mock user account for a 'nonexistent user' test case; the literal '123456' is dummy test data, not a real credential guarding any production system.

This is a test fixture in a *.test.ts file where a fake TOTP secret is used to construct a test case for a nonexistent user; it is not a credential protecting any real system or reachable from untrusted input.

This is a unit test file assigning a literal JWT string to a mocked request object's cookie field purely to set up test fixtures for retrieveLoggedInUser(); it is not a credential embedded in production code or reachable from untrusted input.

This is unit test code that temporarily sets process.env.ALCHEMY_API_KEY to a dummy 'test-key' string to test checkIfEnvironmentVariableExists(), restoring the original value in an after() hook; it is not a real credential and not reachable in production code.

req.body.id is passed unsanitized as the MongoDB query filter (and req.body.message as the update value), so an attacker can inject query operators (e.g. {"$ne": null}) to match and modify reviews they don't own; only authentication is checked, not ownership or input shape, making this the classic NoSQL injection pattern this Juice Shop challenge is built to demonstrate.

req.body.id is passed unsanitized directly into the MongoDB query as _id with no ownership check binding it to the authenticated user, so any authenticated user can forge updates to reviews they don't own, and unvalidated query-key input opens the door to NoSQL query-structure injection (e.g., operator injection) since _id is not cast/validated as a scalar ObjectId.

This is the Juice Shop 'correct' code-fix variant (filename suffix _correct) for the NoSQL injection challenge: it validates that req.body.id is a string before using it in the query filter, which is exactly the mitigation that closes the classic operator-injection vector (e.g. passing {"$ne":null} as id). The unchecked req.body.message is only used as a $set value (data), not as query/filter logic, so it doesn't enable query-structure injection.

req.body.id flows unsanitized into the MongoDB filter as _id and req.body.message flows into $set without validation, so a caller can pass a NoSQL operator object (e.g. {"$ne": null}) instead of a plain id to match/update documents outside the intended scope; there's also no ownership check tying the review being updated to the authenticated user.

This is the Juice Shop code-fix challenge's 'correct' fixed version, which explicitly scopes the update to `author: user.data.email` — an attacker can no longer forge reviews belonging to other users, which was the original vulnerability. While req.body.id is still passed directly into the Mongo filter (a residual NoSQL-operator-injection surface), any manipulation is confined to the authenticated user's own documents, so it is not a genuine cross-user access-control or injection defect as flagg

The code blacklists only the $ne operator on req.body.id before using it directly as a MongoDB query filter, but other NoSQL operators (e.g. $gt, $regex, $exists) remain unfiltered, allowing an attacker to pass an object instead of a scalar id and manipulate the query logic — a classic incomplete-denylist NoSQL injection.

WalletModel.findOne is queried using req.body.UserId directly with no check that it matches the authenticated session user, letting any caller retrieve another user's wallet balance (an IDOR/broken-access-control defect); the CWE-943 injection label is likely a mis-classification since Sequelize's default operator handling wouldn't allow query-operator injection here, but the underlying unauthorized-data-access defect is genuine.

The code calls `email?.toString()` before placing it in the Sequelize `where` clause, which coerces any object-based injection payload (e.g. `{$ne: ...}` parsed from a query string) into the literal string "[object Object]" rather than allowing it to be interpreted as a query operator, neutralizing the classic NoSQL/operator-injection vector this CWE targets.

This uses Sequelize's parameterized `where` object syntax (findOne), not raw/string-concatenated queries, so req.params.id and req.body.UserId are bound as literal equality values rather than being able to alter query structure — Sequelize does not treat plain JSON-supplied objects as Op operators by default. The real weakness here is trusting client-supplied UserId for authorization (an IDOR/broken-access-control issue), which is a different class than CWE-943 injection.

The `email` value is passed unsanitized/untyped directly into a Sequelize `where` clause; without visible validation that it's a scalar string, an attacker who controls the JSON body can pass an object with operator keys to alter query semantics (classic NoSQL/ORM injection sink). Source validation for `email` isn't shown in the excerpt, so this is a plausible but not fully confirmed true positive.

The reported line only performs a lookup of a bearer token in an in-memory authenticatedUsers store; it does not construct or issue any URL/HTTP request, so there is no SSRF vector present. This is a mis-classification of the CWE-918 rule against unrelated code.

lastLoginIp is passed as a value to Sequelize's ORM `update()` method with a fixed, hardcoded field name, not used to build a raw query string or dynamic query object key, so there's no injection vector into query logic even though the value itself is only conditionally sanitized.

req.params.id is always a plain string extracted from the URL path segment (Express does not allow path params to become objects), so it cannot inject NoSQL/Sequelize operators like $ne into the `where` clause; Sequelize binds it as a literal value. The real issue here is a missing authorization check (IDOR, CWE-639), not query injection.

The filter only blocks forward slashes, so a single '..' still passes and escapes one directory level, and on Windows-hosted deployments Node's path.resolve treats backslashes as separators, letting an attacker use '..\..\..\' sequences to fully traverse outside logs/ since there is no backslash check or canonical-path containment check after resolution.

The check only rejects filenames containing '/', but path.resolve also treats backslashes as separators on Windows and a single '..' segment (which contains no slash) still climbs directories, so an attacker-controlled params.file can escape the encryptionkeys/ directory and reach arbitrary files.

req.body.captchaId is passed unvalidated directly into the Sequelize `where` clause; if it's an object (e.g. {"$ne": -1}) rather than a scalar, it can be interpreted as a query operator, letting an attacker match an arbitrary captcha record and bypass the intended captchaId binding since they already control both captchaId and captcha in the same request.

req.params.id is always a plain string extracted from the URL path (Express route params cannot be nested objects), and it's passed into a Sequelize `where` equality clause which parameterizes the value — there's no way to inject operators like $ne/$gt as could happen with req.body or req.query, so this is not exploitable NoSQL/SQL injection.

This is a Sequelize (SQL) ORM `where` clause with a fixed key (`id`) and parameter-bound values, not a raw/dynamic NoSQL query construction; modern Sequelize disables string-based operator aliases by default, so req.body.UserId can't inject query operators here. The real defect in this route is a missing authorization check (any user can upgrade another user's account via UserId), which is a broken-access-control/IDOR issue, not the query-injection class CWE-943 describes.

toUrl is user-controlled via query.to and passed to res.redirect after only an allowlist check (isRedirectAllowed) whose prefix-based matching (isUnintendedRedirect using startsWith) is known to be bypassable, and the code even has a built-in challenge that fires when the redirect target isn't one of the intended allowlisted URLs, confirming this is the app's deliberately exploitable open-redirect scenario.

This is Sequelize's user.update() with a single named field (username) bound as a SQL parameter, not a NoSQL query object, so there's no operator-injection vector for CWE-943; the actual weakness here (if any) is missing CSRF protection on the profile-update endpoint (CWE-352), not query/data injection.

The blacklist only rejects forward slashes, so backslash-based traversal sequences (e.g. '..\..\..\etc\passwd') bypass the check entirely on Windows where path.resolve treats backslash as a separator, and the check doesn't normalize/validate the resolved path stays within 'ftp/quarantine/'. This is the well-known incomplete Juice Shop quarantine bypass, a genuine path-traversal defect by design.

req.body.id is passed unsanitized directly into a MongoDB query filter, allowing NoSQL operator injection (e.g. {$ne: null} or {$gt: ''}) to match/update arbitrary reviews; this is the known OWASP Juice Shop noSqlReviewsChallenge/forgedReviewChallenge intentional vulnerability.

The preceding try/catch (lines 77-83) indicates `key` was already used in an earlier lookup (e.g., against a registry of known code-challenge keys) that throws/handles errors for invalid keys, so by line 89 `key` is likely constrained to a whitelisted set of challenge identifiers rather than raw attacker-controlled path input. Without seeing the actual origin/validation of `key`, however, this can't be fully confirmed from the shown code.

yaml.load() here parses a static, bundled YAML file from data/static/codefixes/ shipped with the application, not attacker-supplied YAML content; while `key` may influence the file path, the file contents themselves are not untrusted data, so there's no deserialization-of-untrusted-data vector even though js-yaml's default schema is used.

req.params.id from Express route params is always a plain string (never a nested object), so it cannot inject NoSQL operators, and Sequelize's `where` clause parameterizes the value rather than concatenating raw query syntax.

This is the OWASP Juice Shop likeProductReviews route where the review `id` from user-controlled input flows unsanitized into MongoDB findOne/update queries, enabling NoSQL query injection (e.g. via operator objects like $ne/$gt) since the id is not shown being validated or cast to a safe type before use.

The authorization check relies on `req.body.UserId`, an attacker-controlled value, being combined into the Sequelize `where` clause rather than deriving the user id from an authenticated session; a client can supply an arbitrary UserId (or, if Sequelize's legacy string operator aliases are enabled, an operator object like `{$ne: null}`) to alter query logic and access another user's address, defeating the intended access control.

The flagged line is simple arithmetic (dividing array length by mipmap count) inside a DDS texture parser; there is no file path construction, file system access, or path traversal potential anywhere near this code, so the CWE-22 classification is a mis-tagging by the tool.

The query parameter 'email' comes from loggedInUser.data.email, which is derived from the authenticated session (looked up via a token), not from raw user-controlled request input passed as an object; there's no attacker-controlled query object here to inject NoSQL operators through.

When reflectedXssChallenge is enabled, id is only truncated (not sanitized) via utils.trunc and then interpolated directly into a MongoDB $where JavaScript string, allowing NoSQL/JS injection; even in the default branch the regex-based sanitization is an ad-hoc denylist rather than a safe parameterized query, so untrusted req.params.id can break out of the string context.

The path segment pdfFile is server-generated (`order_${hash}-${randomHex}.pdf`) from a hash of the authenticated user's email and a random hex string, not from any request parameter, so there is no attacker-controlled path traversal input reaching path.join.

The username field, which is user-controlled and stored/retrieved from the user object, is regex-matched and then substring-extracted content is passed directly to eval() with no sanitization, allowing arbitrary JavaScript code execution if an attacker sets their username to a crafted `#{...}` payload.

When the noSqlCommandChallenge flag is enabled, req.params.id is only truncated to 40 chars (not sanitized) and concatenated directly into a MongoDB $where clause, allowing NoSQL/JS injection; this is Juice Shop's intentionally vulnerable code path for that exact challenge, so the injection is genuinely reachable and exploitable in that branch.

The query value `updatedEmail` is derived by calling `.replace()` on `loggedInUser.data.email`, which guarantees it is a plain string (not an object), so NoSQL operator injection (e.g. `{$ne: ...}`) is not possible; the underlying email also comes from the authenticated session, not raw attacker-controlled JSON.

updatedEmail is derived from the authenticated session record (security.authenticatedUsers.get(token).data.email), a server-side stored string, then passed through .replace() which always yields a plain string — not attacker-controlled JSON/object input that could inject Mongo query operators. There is no direct user-supplied object reaching the query filter here.

The `file` parameter is only checked for an allowlisted extension (or an exact filename) and has null bytes stripped, but no path-traversal sanitization (e.g. stripping `../` or rejecting slashes/backslashes) is applied before `path.resolve('ftp/', file)`, allowing a payload like `../package.json\x00.md` to escape the ftp directory and read arbitrary files — this is the classic poison-null-byte path traversal (this is Juice Shop's intentionally vulnerable fileServer route).

The rate limiter's keyGenerator trusts the client-supplied X-Forwarded-For header as the rate-limit key instead of relying solely on the validated req.ip, letting an attacker rotate that header per request to bypass rate limiting and brute-force the password reset endpoint.

product.name is concatenated into a plain string (tweetText) used to build a Twitter-intent URL parameter, not written into the DOM via innerHTML or Angular bypassSecurityTrust; no rendering sink is shown, so this is not a demonstrated XSS.

The redirect target is built from basketService.hostServer (a service-configured API host, not user input) and orderId (the current order's own ID), not from attacker-controllable query parameters or referrer data; without evidence that either value is attacker-influenced, this isn't a genuine open-redirect sink.

This line only passes a string into Angular's Router.navigate queryParams, which Angular serializes/encodes into the URL; there is no DOM sink (innerHTML, bypassSecurityTrust*, etc.) here, so no XSS occurs at this location regardless of message content.
The 'url' field is a hardcoded string literal pointing to a fixed blockchain.info address, not derived from user-controllable input, so there is no open-redirect vector here.

The flagged line reads a basket id from sessionStorage and URL-encodes a user-entered coupon value to call applyCoupon; there is no hardcoded credential, secret, or password anywhere in this code, so CWE-798 is a mis-classification.

This line only assigns a query parameter to a local string field; no parsing, casting, or control-flow decision is made on unvalidated structure here, so there's no missing-validation defect at this location. Any real risk (e.g. unsanitized data rendered via bypassSecurityTrustHtml on line 48, or IDOR via unauthenticated order lookup) lies elsewhere, not in this simple property read.

bypassSecurityTrustHtml explicitly disables Angular's built-in HTML sanitization for a string built via template literal from results.data[0].orderId, which originates from a backend response tied to attacker-influenced order data; if that field is ever attacker-controlled it renders as raw HTML/script in the DOM.

This line only stores a token value in localStorage; it does not render or inject unsanitized data into the DOM, so it is not a cross-site scripting sink. Storing tokens in localStorage is a credential-storage concern (closer to CWE-522) rather than CWE-79, making this a mis-classification.

The redirectUrl is taken directly from an untrusted query parameter and used later to redirect the user after login (this is the OWASP Juice Shop login flow), without validation against an allowlist of internal paths, allowing an attacker to craft a login link that redirects victims to an arbitrary external site.

config.application.welcomeBanner.message is assigned directly to this.message from server-supplied application configuration; the hardcoded default value at line 28 contains raw HTML markup, strongly implying the component template renders `message` via [innerHTML] without sanitization, making it a stored XSS sink if the config value is attacker-influenced (a known intentional vulnerability pattern in OWASP Juice Shop).

The webhook URL defaults to and is intended to come from process.env.SOLUTIONS_WEBHOOK (server-side config), not from untrusted user input; nothing in the shown code indicates an attacker can control the `webhook` parameter passed to notify().

This is Juice Shop challenge-tracking code that merely checks (via regex match and a security.isRedirectAllowed check) whether the client-supplied data string looks like a successful exploit of the SSRF/redirect challenge; it does not itself issue any outbound request using untrusted input, so no SSRF occurs here.

This is server-side challenge-verification logic (OWASP Juice Shop) that merely checks whether client-supplied data contains a known XSS payload string via utils.contains(); it never reflects, renders, or outputs the data to any page or client, so there is no XSS sink present.

The value is coerced via Number() before being concatenated into the $where clause, so only a numeric (or NaN) literal can ever reach the query string — no attacker-controlled string/JS payload can be injected. This file also lives under data/static/codefixes/, Juice Shop's set of multiple-choice fix candidates for its coding challenges, not a live production code path.

User/tool-controlled product id is concatenated unsanitized into a MongoDB `$where` clause, which is evaluated as JavaScript server-side, enabling NoSQL/code injection; this is the exact vulnerable pattern the 'chatbotGreedyInjectionChallenge' demo is built to illustrate. The `Number(Id)` (capital I) looks like a possible typo versus the destructured `id`, which is worth flagging separately, but the sink itself is a genuine unsanitized-concatenation-into-$where injection.

The query uses parameterized binding ($1, $2 with a `bind` array) rather than string concatenation/interpolation, so user-supplied email/password cannot break out of the query — this is the fixed/safe variant of the login SQL injection challenge, not a vulnerable sink.

The system prompt embeds a 'CONFIDENTIAL - INTERNAL ONLY' business rule that the LLM is told never to proactively disclose, but since it lives in the model context it can be exfiltrated via prompt injection/jailbreak, exposing internal policy to end users — this is the intentional vulnerability in Juice Shop's chatbot greedy injection challenge.

Line 66 builds a MongoDB $where clause via string concatenation, which executes arbitrary JavaScript server-side and is a textbook NoSQL injection sink — this exact pattern is CWE-943 regardless of framework. The apparent `Id`/`id` typo (undefined `Id` instead of the destructured `id`) looks like a bug in this particular sample, but the unsafe query-construction pattern itself is the defect being flagged and is present as written.

This uses Sequelize's object-based `where` clause syntax, which parameterizes values automatically rather than concatenating raw SQL strings, so `req.body.UserId` cannot be used to alter query structure. There is no raw query or string interpolation present in the shown code.

WalletModel.increment uses Sequelize's ORM query builder with a fixed column name ('balance') and where clause built from an object literal; req.body.balance and req.body.UserId are passed as parameterized values, not concatenated into raw SQL, so standard Sequelize injection protections apply. The real issue here is a business-logic flaw (unauthenticated/unchecked balance manipulation), not SQL injection.

The query uses Sequelize's object-based `where` clause with a parameterized value (email is coerced via `.toString()`), which Sequelize binds as a parameter rather than concatenating into raw SQL — this is not vulnerable to SQL injection. The `.toString()` call also neutralizes attempts to inject Sequelize operators via a query-object payload.

The handler trusts req.body.UserId from the client to select which user's payment cards to return instead of deriving the user identity from the authenticated session, allowing any caller to enumerate other users' stored card records (masked, but still an IDOR/broken access control issue).

The query uses Sequelize's ORM `findAll({ where: { UserId: ... } })`, which generates parameterized SQL rather than concatenating user input into a query string, so this is not exploitable as SQL injection despite the tainted `req.body.UserId` value.
Line 44 is a database update call with no logging statement involved; CWE-532 (sensitive data in log files) does not apply here since nothing is written to a log at this line.

The code reads walletAddress from the request body and checks membership in a server-side set with no authentication step at all — there's no alternate-name/alias bypass pattern here, just an absent identity/signature check, so it doesn't match CWE-289's specific mechanism even if a broader authorization concern could be argued.

The handler accepts a new password from query.new and only checks for non-empty and match with the repeat field (lines 19-25); there is no password strength/complexity validation before it is used to update the user's credentials, which is exactly the weak-password-requirements defect CWE-521 describes.

The line only sends a generic, translated user-facing message ('Current password is not correct.') indicating a password mismatch; it does not leak stack traces, exception details, or internal system information that CWE-209 targets.

When the httpHeaderXssChallenge is enabled, the code skips security.sanitizeSecure() and stores the raw, attacker-controlled `true-client-ip` header directly to the user's lastLoginIp field; if this value is later rendered unescaped (e.g., in an admin view), it enables stored XSS, matching the known Juice Shop httpHeaderXssChallenge design.

The route fetches a basket by an attacker-controlled `id` param and returns its contents without any authorization check that the basket belongs to the requesting user; the `challengeUtils.solveIf` call merely detects exploitation of this flaw for a CTF challenge rather than preventing it, so any authenticated user can enumerate `id` to view other users' baskets (IDOR/broken access control).

tmpToken from req.body is cryptographically verified via security.verify() (JWT signature check) before security.decode() is even invoked, thanks to short-circuit &&; if verification fails, decode never runs and the destructure throws inside the try/catch. This is proper validation of untrusted input, not a missing-validation flaw.

The code re-authenticates via password comparison, checks 2FA isn't already set up, verifies the setupToken's signature with security.verify() before decoding it, validates the token 'type' field, and then checks the OTP against the embedded secret — this is a properly layered validation chain, not a missing-validation defect as shown.

req.body.walletAddress is added to an in-memory Set with no downstream sink shown (no eval, no query, no template injection); lack of format validation alone doesn't constitute an exploitable defect from the code shown.
The expression passed to eval() is built entirely from server-generated random numbers and a fixed operator list, with no user-controlled input; there is no injection vector here.

quantity is never validated to be a positive integer before this check; a negative quantity satisfies both the limitPerUser and stock comparisons, letting an attacker bypass per-user limits and stock checks and even manipulate basket totals (classic Juice Shop negative-quantity business-logic flaw).

Line 45 is Juice Shop's internal challenge-tracking callback (challengeUtils.solveIf) that merely flags whether a CTF challenge was solved; the actual authorization decision is already made and enforced by the strict-ish check and 401 response on lines 37-38 before this code runs, so the loose '!=' here has no security effect.

This is a plain document insert (product, message, author fields) into a collection store — user input becomes stored data, not executed code, so there's no code generation/execution path here. This may represent a genuine stored-XSS or NoSQL-injection concern (different CWEs), but it is not CWE-94 code injection.

res.jsonp(response) reflects the user-controlled req.query.callback parameter as the JSONP function name without validation/sanitization, allowing injection of arbitrary script content into the response (classic JSONP callback XSS).

Payment validation only occurs when paymentMode is 'card' (and presumably 'wallet' earlier); any other paymentMode value skips validation entirely and reaches user.update granting deluxe role for free. This is confirmed by the challenge check on line 45 which explicitly detects paymentMode values other than 'wallet'/'card' as the exploit condition, showing the missing-authorization path is real and reachable.

The path segment comes from application config (config.get('application.promotion.subtitles')), not from user-supplied request input, so there is no attacker-controlled path traversal vector reachable at runtime; it's a hardcoded/admin-configured value with a safe default.

The path segment comes from server-side config (config.get), not from user-controlled request input, so there's no attacker-controlled path traversal vector reachable through this function; it's also passed through utils.extractFilename before concatenation.

req.body.username is persisted to the user record without any sanitization or encoding; this is the known OWASP Juice Shop stored-XSS sink where the unsanitized username is later rendered unescaped in the profile view, enabling stored XSS.

This is a straightforward Sequelize ORM `.create()` call with plain data fields (caption, imagePath, UserId); there is no code evaluation, template rendering, or dynamic code construction from user input, so CWE-94 (Code Injection) does not apply to this line.

UserId is taken directly from req.body without verifying it matches the authenticated user (e.g., req.user.id), allowing any authenticated user to create memory records attributed to an arbitrary other UserId — a broken access control / IDOR issue rather than deriving ownership from the session.

req.params.id is concatenated unsanitized into a MongoDB $where clause, which is evaluated as JavaScript server-side; the code path only truncates length (utils.trunc) without escaping or validating content, allowing NoSQL/code injection via crafted id values.

The user is authenticated and loggedInUser.data.id is available, but the MemoryModel query uses the attacker-controlled req.body.UserId instead of the authenticated user's id, letting any logged-in user retrieve another user's memories by supplying an arbitrary UserId.

allOrders() has no authentication/authorization check before returning all orders via ordersCollection.find(), unlike the adjacent handler which scopes results to the logged-in user's email — this exposes every user's order data to any caller who can reach the route.

req.params.id is untrusted and JSON.parse'd directly into a Sequelize where clause, allowing an attacker to inject an object (e.g. Sequelize operators) instead of a scalar id, altering query semantics; CWE-94 is a mislabel but this is a genuine injection vulnerability reachable from user input.
